transcript.transcript: When they come back, the men were all drunk. A couple of guys got into a fight and it turned out to be a real melee. All the men got into us. We made the women so mad that they all threw all the men overboard. They grabbed them all bodily and threw them all overboard. Until they sobered up and they brought them home, soap and wet. But that was, I remember this fella telling me, he said, boy, I had to run for my life because they thought I was drunk too. You're going to throw them overboard. So That was called the Palouse Picnic. That was one of their famous ones. And then there was others that they had. On Sundays, whenever they had a cruise with Amelia Wheaton, that was the boat that was named after Colonel Wheaton's 12-year-old daughter. And it was built by Peter Sorensen, who was the first boat builder on Coeur d'Alene Lake. And that was built in 1882. But They would take the boat up to Cataldo Mission on Sundays for take families out for cruises. During the week, they would haul hay from the Cataldo Mission flats. They'd go up there and cut hay and haul it from there to Fort Sherman to feed their horses. So it served a good purpose all the way around. It was used to transport supplies. And of course later on when The lumber camps opened up, they used it to haul supplies to the lumber camps too. In those days, they had docks along the river or along the lakeshore. They would haul the supplies and put them on the dock and then they'd come down from the lumber camps with their pack horses and pack the supplies back up to lumber camps. And that was done pretty regularly. I mean, those guys had to have food and supplies all the time. And that was the reason for a lot of those big stern holders, because they were supplying, a lot of them were supplying a lot of the lumber camps from the warehouses that were here in town. Did people actually just stand on the banks and wave down those riverboats as they were coming to catch a ride? Oh no, they had docks that you could catch them on. You'd stand on a dock and they always had, they knew about what time they'd be around. They had regular, regular routes that people would expect to see them on. Mail routes, they delivered their mail that way, they delivered their... It took a while to see, there wasn't many roads around the lake in those days. They were all packed horses and packed trains. So it had to be done that way. It was the only way you could get supplies and mail and things like that to each of the lumber camps. You too. I understand they even ran cattle up on this. Oh yeah, I've got a picture of loading some horses on one of the stern wheelers from the dock. I used that in my slide talk when I talked to the kids in the history class. So they can see just how big that boat was. It could carry as many as five or six horses too. Those are quite the thing. Well I guess we could wrap this up and I sure appreciate you doing this for me. This is excellent. I've learned a lot myself. The gentleman that I have just been talking to is Marvin Jones. His age is 55 years old. He was born in 1927 in South Dakota. He's lived in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho most of his life. He's an excellent historian and his occupation is a barber. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

lines.line: > The interview begins with a story about a past event known as the Palouse Picnic. During this event, a group of men became drunk and got into a fight, resulting in a chaotic scene. The women at the picnic became so angry that they threw the men overboard into the water to sober them up.
** Palouse Picnic, drunk, melee, fight, women, men overboard **
